frame_analyses.description: At 9 seconds, both instruments already in field and actively engaged near drain. Serial number visible on left instrument. Needle and suture visible, instruments positioned close to drain surface. Quick entry.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Very fast instrument entry and needle engagement — comparable to post-practice video. Minimal idle time before task begins.

frame_analyses.description: At 34 seconds, needle has been passed through the drain. Left instrument (serrated jaws visible) holding suture near drain. Long suture thread extending upward. Needle is being retrieved after passage through tissue.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture placement progressing well. The view shows needle clearly passed through the drain tissue. Instruments working with coordination.

frame_analyses.description: At 51 seconds, both instruments working close together at drain surface. Suture thread visible. Completing passage through second mark or finalizing suture placement.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Suture placement phase approaching completion. Instruments are coordinated and working efficiently near the drain marks.

frame_analyses.description: At 77 seconds, clear wrapping motion for first throw. Left instrument has suture looped around it. Right instrument holding free end. Suture anchored at drain. Classic throw wrapping setup.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Surgeon's knot wrapping in progress. The looping pattern around the left instrument appears controlled and deliberate.

frame_analyses.description: At 111 seconds, curved needle clearly visible above the right instrument. Both instruments working near drain. Suture thread visible. Wrapping for second throw. Instrument positions appear swapped from first throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Hand positions appear to have changed — right instrument is now more prominent in the upper position, suggesting a hand switch occurred. The curved needle is still on the suture, visible in frame.

frame_analyses.description: At 137 seconds, instruments near drain with suture loop visible. Third throw wrapping or tightening in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Third and final throw. The pace through the throw phases has been consistent and controlled.

frame_analyses.description: At 163 seconds, a BARE HAND is visible in the frame — the trainee or proctor is manually lifting the Penrose drain edge to inspect the knot and slit closure. The knot is clearly visible on the drain with dark suture thread tied securely. The drain slit edges are well-approximated under the knot. Short suture tails visible. Drain remains attached to suture block.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Manual inspection reveals GOOD slit closure with the knot holding the drain edges together. The knot appears compact and secure. Suture tails are appropriately short. This confirms the task was completed successfully before this inspection frame. Actual task completion was approximately 155-160 seconds.

frame_analyses.description: Final frame at 171 seconds. Drain has been set back down after inspection. Instruments fully withdrawn. Task complete.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Post-inspection view. The drain appears settled back on the suture block after manual review.

knot_assessments.notes: Frame 10 shows looping around the left instrument for the surgeon's knot. The double-wrap pattern is consistent with a surgeon's knot. Confidence moderate.
Frame 14 shows the right instrument now in the primary wrapping position. Hand switch from left (throw 1) to right (throw 2) appears to have occurred. Confidence moderate.

Third throw completed by frame 17. The inspection frame (20) confirms the knot appears secure and the slit is well-closed. Manual inspection by hand shows the knot holding firm. Confidence moderate-high due to direct inspection evidence.

technique_summary: This is the third video in the training progression and shows the best performance to date. Completion time of approximately 158 seconds represents continued improvement (pre: 198s, post-practice: 170s, post-2: 158s). The needle loading phase remains fast at ~13 seconds. The most notable feature of this video is the manual inspection at frame 20 (163 seconds) where a hand lifts the drain edge to check the knot — this confirms good slit closure and a compact, secure-appearing knot with short suture tails. All three throws were completed with apparent hand switching. Suture placement time remains the largest phase at 40 seconds, representing the primary opportunity for further time savings. The throw phases were each completed in 25-30 seconds. Cutting time improved to approximately 18 seconds.
improvement_suggestions: Suture placement remains the longest phase at ~40 seconds — practice needle driving technique to target 25 seconds
Individual throw times of 25-30 seconds each still have room for improvement — target 15-20 seconds through smoother wrapping technique
Continue maintaining the fast needle loading pace (13 seconds is approaching efficient)
Cutting phase at 18 seconds is improved — continue refining for 10-15 second target
strengths: Continued time improvement across all three sessions (198s → 170s → 158s)
Excellent final result confirmed by manual inspection — tight knot, good slit closure, short suture tails
Needle loading speed maintained at efficient level (13 seconds)
All three throws completed correctly with apparent hand switching
Drain not avulsed — consistent good tissue handling across all three attempts
Suture cutting time improved from 25s to 18s
Knot security appears high based on visual inspection evidence

key_improvements: Drain closure: partial → complete → complete (confirmed by inspection)
